pages.text: At the core of QMD are Accelerator Multiblocks. There are many different types: $(l:multiblocks/accelerators/linear_accelerator)Linear$(/l), $(l:multiblocks/accelerators/ring_accelerator)Synchrotron$(/l),  $(l:multiblocks/accelerators/decelerator)Decelerator$(/l), $(l:multiblocks/accelerators/Beam_diverter)Beam Diverter$(/l), $(l:multiblocks/accelerators/beam_splitter)Beam Splitter$(/l) and $(l:multiblocks/accelerators/mass_spectrometer)Mass Spectrometer$(/l). All these do different things but use the same basic principles and materials. Here we go into the basic principles.
A whole bunch of accelerators in a system doing a whole bunch of stuff.

pages.title: Cooling
pages.text: Accelerators need to be cooled as they and the environment produce heat. If they overheat while operating, some of the overheating components will $(l)explode$().$(br2)To cool an accelerator you need to pipe in a cold coolant and pipe out a hot coolant. Each coolant has a different temperature; this determines the minimum temperature your accelerator can reach.

For example, Liquid Helium is 4 Kelvin (K), Liquid Nitrogen is 70 K. The valid coolants and their temperatures can be seen in JEI. $(br)To pipe coolant in and out of an accelerator you need at least 2 Accelerator Coolant Vents, one in input mode and one in output mode. The mode can be switched using the Nuclearcraft multitool. $(br2)Most accelerators need coolants that can either be created in a $(l:multiblocks/other_multiblocks/liquefier)liquefier$(/l) or a supercooler.

pages.title: Construction
pages.text: The outside (casing) of all accelerators are made out of Accelerator Casing or Accelerator Glass. In the casing you need: $(li)The accelerator's controller$(li)at least one energy port$(li)At least one input coolant vent $(li)At least one output coolant vent$(br2)You can also have optional Redstone or computer ports.

Inside each accelerator, is a connected line of Beam Blocks that the particles will travel through. Around this beam can be 3 different types of $(l)component structures$(): $(l)Radio Frequency (RF) Cavities$(), $(l)Dipole Magnets$() and $(l)Quadrupole Magnets$(). Around these component structures coolers are placed to cool the whole accelerator.

Each structure contributes to a different stat of the accelerator: RF Cavities add voltage; Dipole Magnets add dipole strength and Quadrupole Magnets add quadrupole strength. The amount each structure adds can be seen on the tooltip of the block that makes it. General stats like power efficiency, heat produced and base energy also listed on the blocks' tooltip is also the amount each structure gives $(l)not each block$().

RF cavities are constructed from 8 RF Cavity blocks of the same type in a ring around the accelerator beam as shown above. RF cavities cannot be directly next to each other, requiring at least a block of space between them.

RF cavities increase the accelerating voltage of the accelerator and thus the $(l:particles/introduction#energy)energy$(/l) of the resulting particle out the end. The accelerating voltage of each RF Cavity is determined by the material of the cavity's blocks. Shown to the left is a RF cavity made of niobium titanium.
Quadrupole Magnets are constructed from 4 Accelerator Electromagnets of the same type around an Accelerator Beam as shown above.

Quadrupoles increase the $(l:particles/introduction#focus)focus$(/l) (basically the confinement) of the particle beam. The focus is used to tell how far a beam can travel. If the beam travels too far in an accelerator it will not output. So, more quadrupoles are needed to be added to compensate for the $(l:particles/introduction#focus_loss)loss in focus$(/l). The strength of a quadrupole is determined by the type of electromagnet used to create it. Shown to the left is a quadrupole made of copper.

Dipole Magnets are created by placing an Accelerator Electromagnet of the same type above and below a beam and filling the rest of the 3x3x3 space around that beam with Accelerator yokes. Shown above is a Synchrotron accelerator with 5 Dipoles.
Dipoles can have multiple beam blocks coming in and out of them replacing the yokes. Shown below is the same Synchrotron with the yokes removed to show where the beams blocks are.

Dipoles can also be oriented vertically in $(l:multiblocks/accelerators/Beam_diverter)Beam Diverters$(/l) and $(l:multiblocks/accelerators/beam_splitter)Beam Splitters$(/l) to allow the particles to be in and outputted up and down. Example below is 2 beam diverters with vertical dipoles.
The strength of each dipole is the strength of the electromagnet used to create it. Dipoles bend the path of beams so they must be placed at beam corners and intersections.

pages.title: Coolers
pages.text: Each RF cavity block and electromagnet produce heat while operating. To get rid of this heat coolers are placed inside the accelerator. They only work if their required rules are met. These rules are shown on the tool tips of each cooler. You can check if coolers are in valid positions by sneak right clicking the controller of a formed accelerator with the $(l:multiblocks/multitool)multitool$(/l).
Shown above is a $(l:multiblocks/accelerators/linear_accelerator)Linear Accelerator$(/l) with a quadrupole and RF cavity with coolers placed around them.

pages.title: Operation
pages.text: There are a few things you should know about how to operate accelerators before building or turning them on. They are Power, Heating, Coolant, Focus and Control.

pages.title: Power
pages.text: Hovering over the Power bar (left) of the gui will show you the power stored in the accelerator and the power used by the accelerator when on. The power used can be calculated as $(l)P=p/╬Ą$() where $(l)p$() is sum of all the component structure's base power and $(l)╬Ą$() is the average component efficiency. The percentage in brackets is $(l)1/╬Ą$() which is how much of the sum of base powers is used.

pages.title: Heating
pages.text: There are two sources of heating in an accelerator. External heating from the warm environment, which depends on the size of the accelerator and is always present, and internal heating. This heat comes from the component structures in the accelerator and is only present when the accelerator is turned on.

Hovering over the heat bar (middle) of the gui will show you the heat stored in the accelerator, the Cooling: the amount of cooling the coolers provide, the Current Heating: the amount of heat currently generated, the Maximum Heating: the max amount of heat the accelerator can possibly generated (if your cooling is greater than this and the accelerator has a constant supply of coolant then it will never overheat),
the Maximum External Heating: the max amount of external heat from the environment (this is already included in Maximum Heating).

To calculate the maximum heating it is the sum of the internal and external heating. The external heating is $(br)$(l)HŌéæ=╬║A(TŌéæ-TŌéÉ)$() where $(l)TŌéæ$() is the environment temperature this is 293 K, $(l)TŌéÉ$() is the accelerators temperature, $(l)╬║$() is thermal conductivity (default config is 0.0025) and A is the surface area of the accelerator. The maximum External heating is therefore when $(l)TŌéÉ$() = 0 K. The Internal Heating is the sum of all the component block's heat generation values.

pages.title: Coolant
pages.text: Hovering over the coolant bar (right) of the gui will show you the amount of coolant stored, the maximum rate coolant can be used and the maximum amount of hot coolant that can be produced. The Accelerator's coolant tanks (both input and output) can be cleared by holding shift in the gui and pressing the button that appears. The type of components you use in the accelerator determines its maximum operating temperature.

The temperature of the coolant used must be below this for the accelerator to cool down below its maximum operating temperature. If the accelerator temperature rises above its maximum operating temperature while running, then some of the $(l)$(c)overheating components will explode$().

pages.title: Focus
pages.text: The output focus can be calculated using $(br)$(l)f = fŌéĆ-fŌéōL+|q|BŌéä$()$(br)where $(l)fŌéĆ$() is the input beam's focus or for a newly created beam from an ion source the starting focus, which is determined by the ion source and is seen on its tool tip. $(l)fŌéō$() is the $(l:particles/introduction#focus_loss)focus loss per block$(), which depends on the particle's charge $(l)q$() and the pu/t of the beam. $(l)L$() is the length of the accelerator and $(l)BŌéä$() is the Quadrupole strength in Tesla
of the accelerator which is the sum of the strengths of each quadrupole.$(br2)The length and quadrupole strength can be seen in the accelerator's gui.

pages.title: Control
pages.text: You can control accelerators in two ways either with redstone signals or if Open Computers is installed with a computer. For redstone control: a redstone signal to the controller or a Redstone Port will try to turn the accelerator on if it can. If it can't there will be an error message in the gui. For computer control the computer needs to be connected to the accelerator through an Accelerator Computer Port.

You can find out more details about specific accelerator control in their sections. You can also see more about accelerator computer control in the $(l:multiblocks/open_computers/introduction#accelerator_control)open computers section$().

pages.title: Redstone Ports
pages.text: Accelerators can have any amount of redstone ports in the casing. Redstone ports can be in either input or output modes. Switching between the modes is done with right clicking with the multitool. In input mode restone ports act as if the redstone signal applied to them went to the accelerator's controller, allowing accelerators to turn on and off and change target energies.

In output mode redstone ports output a redstone signal strength proportional to the fraction the current temperature is to the maximum operating temperature of the accelerator. So, at the maximum operating temperature and above the redstone signal strength is 15, at half the maximum operating temperature the signal strength is 7.
